text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025

children.text: Overall orientation
Mandates and background

20.1	The Economic Commission for Europe (ECE) is responsible for facilitating economic integration and cooperation among its member States and promoting sustainable development and economic prosperity in the ECE region. The mandate derives from the priorities established in relevant General Assembly and Economic and Social Council resolutions and decisions, including on the workplan on reform of ECE and the outcome of the review of the 2005 reform of ECE (resolutions 2006/38 and 2013/1, respectively), and Council resolution 36 (IV). As a regional entity with 56 member States and spanning across three continents, the Commission is uniquely positioned to support transboundary, intraregional and interregional cooperation, to help its member States to take bold, ambitious, accelerated, just and transformative action to advance the implementation of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development and leave no one behind.

20.2	ECE will do so by continuing to leverage its core mandated functions of convener of policy dialogue and consensus builder, with a specific focus on existing and new normative frameworks and standards; adviser and provider of on-demand technical expertise, with the aim of strengthening national capacities; and custodian of legal instruments and regional public goods, including knowledge platforms. The support delivered by ECE to member States is also provided through the regular programme of technical cooperation, Development Account projects, and field and technical cooperation projects supported by voluntary contributions, in order to contribute to the objectives, strategy and results.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

20.3	The overall strategy and proposed programme of work of ECE is grounded in the shared vision and ambition of member States and the peoples of the ECE region for a just and prosperous future for all. It has been formulated in alignment with the commitments outlined in the Pact for the Future and remains anchored in multilateral frameworks, such as the 2030 Agenda, the Fourth International Conference on Financing for Development, the Programme of Action for Landlocked Developing Countries for the Decade 2024–2034 and the New Urban Agenda set forth by Member States to address economic and social challenges through sustained, equitable and people-centred development.

20.4	The strategy of the programme integrates sustainable development approaches and builds on synergies and the expected results of its subprogrammes. It leverages cross-sectoral synergies of the 2030 Agenda and collaboration among the subprogrammes of the ECE secretariat, its intergovernmental architecture and broad expert community for multi-stakeholder engagement and action towards accelerating progress in achieving the Sustainable Development Goals, including through the Regional Forum on Sustainable Development for the ECE Region and the United Nations Forum of Mayors. The programme will continue to provide support to its member States in addressing the economic and environmental challenges of achieving the Goals, with a focus on the demographic, digital and green transitions that have significant catalytic and multiplier effects.

20.5	The execution of the strategy will be realized through: (a) the facilitation of intergovernmental processes to promote consensus and cooperation, enable peer-to-peer exchanges of innovative policy solutions and good practices, and strengthen the existing and establish new norms and standards, as well as legal instruments and frameworks; (b) thought leadership and ensuring that member States have access to timely and quality evidence, including by supporting them in measuring their progress towards the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals, and foresight-based policy and technical advice and recommendations for informed decision-making; and (c) the strengthening of capacities of national institutions to implement norms and standards and commitments outlined in multilateral agreements and ECE legal instruments to which the member States are Parties.

20.6	In 2027, the Commission’s work will continue to focus on a range of issues in the ECE region pertaining to, among others, transboundary, subregional, intraregional and interregional cooperation on economic integration and trade; transport and mobility; environmental protection and the management of natural resources; forests and forest industry; affordable and clean energy; competitiveness and public-private partnerships for sustainable development; emerging social and demographic challenges; and land management.

20.7	ECE plans further strengthening of the secretariat’s operational model by enhancing internal coordination, streamlining internal workflows and processes and reinforcing data-driven and cross-sectoral approaches, in line with action 45 (a) of the Pact for the Future for more agile, timely and impactful support to its member States, and the UN80 Initiative.

20.8	With regard to cooperation with other entities at the global, regional, national and local levels, ECE will build on its close cooperation with international and regional organizations and other regional actors to exchange knowledge, develop joint initiatives and enhance collective impact. Partnerships with the private sector and civil society organizations, think tanks and the academic sector will be strengthened to facilitate ECE policy dialogue and normative work and to engage them in the implementation of the 2030 Agenda by member States.

20.9	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, ECE will continue to lead inter-agency coordination in the region, co-chairing the Regional Collaborative Platform for Europe and Central Asia to strengthen joint action on cross-cutting policy issues at the regional level. Through its strategic partnerships and collaboration with United Nations entities and international financial institutions, including through initiatives such as the United Nations Special Programme for the Economies of Central Asia, ECE will continue to deliver integrated policy advice and regional perspectives globally. At the country level, ECE will collaborate with resident coordinators and United Nations country teams to support the implementation of joint programmes, aligned with national priorities and the United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Frameworks. The programme will also foster interregional, South-South and triangular cooperation.
20.10	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:

(a)	ECE member States continue to actively engage and support sustainable development initiatives and related intergovernmental processes and to prioritize regional and subregional cooperation and integration for shared development outcomes;
(b)	All relevant stakeholders maintain the political will and commitment to multilateral cooperation and the implementation of ECE legal instruments, norms and standards;
(c)	Sufficient resources are available for the implementation of planned activities.

20.11	Guided by its corporate policy and action plan on gender equality and the empowerment of women, ECE integrates a gender perspective into its results, deliverables and operational model that enables their delivery, as appropriate, as indicated, for example, in the programme performance for 2025 under subprogramme 6. In doing so, ECE aims to support member States in achieving commitments outlined under Sustainable Development Goal 5 and targets of other Goals relating to gender equality and women’s empowerment.

20.12	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, ECE will continue to ensure that disability inclusion is mainstreamed into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

20.13	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative. In particular, the tables of deliverables reflect updated deliverables for 2026, in line with the approved programme budget for 2026.

20.14	ECE will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs. This includes, among others, any decisions concerning proposals for strengthening collaboration with the United Nations Conference on Trade and Development, the Department of Economic and Social Affairs and the other regional commissions through joint mechanisms. ECE will jointly explore options for strengthening macroeconomic analysis capacities, with a view to contributing more harmonized analysis and policy support to Member States. ECE will also be guided by the competent intergovernmental organs on the ongoing right-sizing and right-servicing exercise within ECE.

Evaluation activities

20.17	The evaluation planned for 2025 was not completed owing to the lack of required staff capacity and resources. Nevertheless, ECE focused on the implementation of the recommendations from previous evaluations, including the one conducted by the Office of Internal Oversight Services in 2023, which also guided the programme plan for 2027.

20.18	In response to the results of the evaluations referenced above, ECE will focus on strengthening programme planning through a review of subprogramme-specific strategies and results frameworks, encompassing initiatives supported by the regular budget, the regular programme for technical cooperation, the Development Account and voluntary contributions. It will also continue to adjust its modes of programme delivery and operational model, including by leveraging modern technologies and cross-cutting initiatives.
20.19	A programme-level evaluation of the ECE normative function is planned for 2027.
Programme of work
Subprogramme 1 – Environment
Objective
20.20	The objective, to which the subprogramme contributes, is to improve environmental governance and performance for safeguarding the environment and health.
Strategy
20.21	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Undertake environmental performance reviews and environmental assessments and assist member States in the implementation of recommendations and monitoring of the resulting impact on national policies;

(b)	Standardize indicators for evidence-based decision-making and environmental assessments and support member States in generating, disseminating and providing open access to environmental information;

(c)	Provide technical advice to member States and strengthen national capacities, including through the facilitation of peer-to-peer exchanges of good practices, on matters pertaining to (i) environmental policymaking, (ii) environmental performance, (iii) environmental monitoring and assessments, (iv) the mainstreaming of environmental concerns into economic and social development policies, (v) education for sustainable development, (vi) air quality, (vii) water, (viii) industrial safety, (ix) public participation and (x) impact assessment;

(d)	Provide platforms for policy dialogue, consensus-building and advocacy to assist member States in addressing priority and emerging environmental issues and in developing region-wide approaches, including for sustainable and resilient infrastructure, while ensuring effective and inclusive access to information, public participation in decision-making and access to justice.

20.22	In addition, the subprogramme, in its role of secretariat to eight multilateral environmental agreements, will support existing Parties in their implementation, advocate for and facilitate the accession of additional member States and promote synergies among these agreements. Moreover, the subprogramme, in its role of co-custodian for Sustainable Development Goal indicator 6.5.2, will support member States in identifying gaps and opportunities in and advancing cooperation on the protection and use of transboundary water resources.

20.23	Furthermore, in its commitment to drive cross-sectoral, cross-cutting and joint initiatives, the subprogramme will continue to implement the ECE Strategy for Education for Sustainable Development and the Transport, Health and Environment Pan-European Programme.
20.24	The above-mentioned work will help member States to make progress towards achieving Sustainable Development Goals 3, 4, 6, 9, 11–13 and 15–17 and is expected to result in:

(a)	Improved environmental governance and performance, more effective development and implementation of environmental legislation and policies, enhanced integration of environmental concerns into national sectoral policies, and a better response to current and emerging environmental challenges;
(b)	Strengthened implementation of multilateral environmental commitments and their increased geographical coverage.
Programme performance in 2025
Member States apply foresight approaches to develop recommendations for environmental policies to address gaps and emerging issues

20.25	Urbanization, demographic change, digitalization and trends in globalization interact with and may, if not addressed holistically, potentially amplify the adverse effects of climate change and environmental degradation on environmental and socioeconomic systems. The subprogramme, in collaboration with a higher education institution, facilitated science-policy dialogue and developed guidance for environmental policymakers in the region on applying foresight approaches to support the early identification of emerging trends and gaps in sectoral policies that have an impact on the achievement of climate-related commitments and goals. Subsequently, the subprogramme supported member States and enhanced their capacity to identify priority areas for action and formulate coherent environmental policies aimed at gaps and emerging issues.

children.text: Result 3: improved reporting of air emissions data – Proposed programme plan for 2027

20.31	Reporting air emissions data is a prerequisite for assessing progress made by the Parties in meeting their commitments under the Convention on Long-range Transboundary Air Pollution. Furthermore, the reported data support modelling efforts that allow for an assessment of the effectiveness of previously adopted measures and the strengthening of existing and development of new, ambitious policies, strategies and legal instruments pertaining to emissions reduction. The subprogramme has supported Parties in generating and improving the reporting of air emissions data, including by identifying gaps in inventories.
Lessons learned and planned change

20.32	The lesson for the subprogramme was that there were opportunities to further improve data quality and completeness to support the modelling and robustness of the evidence for environmental policymaking. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will enhance its assessments of the data submitted through informative inventory reports and evaluate the effectiveness and robustness of methodologies applied by Parties to collect and process data, with the aim of identifying gaps and challenges, as well as good practices and innovative solutions. Concurrently, it will provide technical advice to and strengthen capacities of the Parties to produce national reports on air emissions.

Subprogramme 2 – Transport
Objective

20.35	The objective, to which the subprogramme contributes, is to advance an intraregionally and interregionally sustainable inland transport (road, rail, inland waterway and intermodality) system by making it safer, cleaner, more efficient, more inclusive and more affordable, both for freight transport and people’s mobility.
Strategy
20.36	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Service the Inland Transport Committee, its 21 working parties, 13 administrative committees and the Economic and Social Council Committee of Experts on the Transport of Dangerous Goods and on the Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labelling of Chemicals and its 2 subcommittees, as well as more than 50 expert networks. It will support the Inland Transport Committee’s coordination of its subsidiary bodies and its broader interregional role, as endorsed in the revised Inland Transport Committee terms of reference adopted by the Council (E/RES/2022/2) and promote implementation of the Inland Transport Committee strategy until 2030 and the Inland Transport Committee Strategy on Reducing Greenhouse-gas Emissions from Inland Transport;

(b)	Service, administer and further develop the international regulatory framework comprising 61 United Nations legal instruments on inland transport that enable the safe, efficient and sustainable movement of people and goods across borders, ensuring that they remain open to accession by all United Nations Member States, when and as appropriate, and that they stimulate economic growth, facilitate trade and support innovation and competitiveness. In this capacity, the subprogramme will:

(i)	Update and promote the application of vehicle regulations covering safety, environmental performance, energy efficiency, automated and electric mobility, and intelligent transport systems, thereby supporting innovation and cleaner fleets;

(ii)	Ensure the continuous updating and global alignment of the regulatory framework governing the safe transport of hazardous substances by road, rail and inland waterways, reducing risks to people, communities and the environment;
(iii)	Advance implementation of United Nations road traffic conventions and vehicle regulation agreements that help to save lives and enhance the safety of all road users;

(iv)	Continue the administration and operations of the Convention on International Transport of Goods under Cover of TIR Carnets (TIR Convention), including the implementation and operations of its electronic procedure (eTIR); the International Convention on the Harmonization of Frontier Controls of Goods; the Convention on the Contract for the International Carriage of Goods by Road and its electronic implementation (eCMR); and the Customs Convention on the Temporary Importation of Commercial Road Vehicles and preparation of its electronic carnets (eCPDs) and related conventions, to enhance supply chain efficiency, lower transaction costs and foster resilient trade connectivity;

(v)	With regard to infrastructure agreements, administer conventions on road, rail, inland waterways, combined transport and related infrastructure to promote modal integration, energy efficiency and resilience;

(c)	Provide an intergovernmental platform for policy dialogue and consensus-building through the annual sessions of the Inland Transport Committee and its subsidiary bodies. The Committee platform reviews emerging challenges, strengthens regional and interregional cooperation and promotes harmonized action through the adoption of strategies, action plans, high-level statements and resolutions that enable the full implementation of the Committee’s mandate;

(d)	Analyse trends and provide technical assistance and capacity-building to enable member States to accede to and implement legal instruments, at the request of member States and contracting Parties within and beyond the ECE region;

(e)	Facilitate horizontal and cross-sectoral initiatives, including implementation of the United Nations Decade of Sustainable Transport (see General Assembly resolution 78/148) and the Transport, Health and Environment Pan-European Programme, and promote synergies among and cooperation with key transport stakeholders, including the Special Envoy of the Secretary-General for Road Safety and the United Nations Road Safety Fund.
20.37	The above-mentioned work will help member States to make progress towards achieving Sustainable Development Goals 3, 5–9 and 11–13 and is expected to result in:

(a)	Enhanced efficiency, interoperability and resilience of inland transport and border-crossing operations through the implementation and digitalization of United Nations legal instruments, leading to more seamless and sustainable flows of goods and people within and across regions;

(b)	Improved road traffic safety and the overall safety of inland transport through strengthened regulatory frameworks and the effective implementation of vehicle and driver standards and regulations;

(c)	Reduced greenhouse gas emissions and environmental impacts from inland transport, accelerated progress towards net-zero emissions by 2050 and the strengthened adaptation and resilience of transport systems, guided by Inland Transport Committee strategies.
Programme performance in 2025
Open-source software enables implementation of annex 11 of the Convention on International Transport of Goods under Cover of TIR Carnets

20.38	A lack of cost-effective and adaptable digital infrastructure has impeded the implementation of annex 11 of the TIR Convention, which governs the transition to fully electronic procedures in accordance with the Convention. To date, direct interconnection to the eTIR international system, the main data exchange hub of the electronic TIR procedure, requires investment of national resources and up to a year to complete. To enable access to the eTIR procedure, the subprogramme designed an open-source, technology-neutral, secure and scalable tool, the eTIR National Application, which can be installed and deployed within two weeks on existing national systems and allows countries with limited information technology capacity to implement paperless cross-border procedures, without the need to invest into costly and lengthy national system redesign. Subsequently, the subprogramme engaged with member States to build their capacity to roll out the tool.

Result 3: accelerated digitalization of legal instruments in inland transport – Proposed programme plan for 2027

20.44	The digitalization of legal instruments pertaining to inland transport helps to advance their implementation by contracting Parties by enhancing the efficiency, safety and security of international transport, which is a critical component of trade facilitation. The subprogramme has promoted accession to United Nations transport conventions, while supporting efforts to modernize procedures governed by these instruments.
Lessons learned and planned change

20.45	The lesson for the subprogramme was that the modernization of national and international legal frameworks and sustained investment in capacity-building, in addition to technical infrastructure, helped to accelerate implementation of electronic procedures under legal instruments. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will prioritize support for contracting Parties in aligning and modernizing national legal frameworks, strengthening institutional coordination and building the capacities of relevant authorities to operationalize electronic procedures in a consistent and sustainable manner. The subprogramme will also develop new and promote the use of existing open-source, technology-neutral and scalable digital tools, including but not limited to the eTIR National Application, the e-CMR conceptual and functional specifications and the digitized signs and signals under the Convention on Road Signs and Signals, with the aim of enabling equitable access of all contracting Parties, including ECE programme countries, to modern transport governance systems.

Subprogramme 3 – Statistics
Objective

20.48	The objective, to which the subprogramme contributes, is to advance official statistics at the national and international levels for evidence-based policymaking and assessing progress towards achieving the Sustainable Development Goals and to ensure the coordination of statistical activities in the ECE region under the Conference of European Statisticians.
Strategy
20.49	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Develop methodological guidelines, recommendations and models to modernize and enhance the quality of official statistics;

(b)	Promote the in-depth discussion and exchange of experience and good practice in official statistics at the Conference of European Statisticians and at expert meetings on social, economic and environment statistics and statistical modernisation;

(c)	Promote the uptake by member States of international statistical standards, guidelines and models and enhance capacities of national statistical offices, including on cross-cutting topics such as statistics for measuring progress against the Sustainable Development Goals, statistical legislation, and the collection, integration and dissemination of data;
(d)	Support the integration of geospatial information into statistics, in partnership with geospatial experts and relevant United Nations entities in the region;
(e)	Maintain and enhance the ECE statistical database, of which it is a custodian.
20.50	The above-mentioned work will help member States make progress towards achieving Sustainable Development Goals 1, 5, 6, 8, 10 and 12–17 and is expected to result in:
(a)	Production by member States of statistics necessary to develop evidence-based policies to advance the achievement of and measure the progress against the Sustainable Development Goals;

(b)	The use by member States of innovative and efficient approaches to conducting censuses that result in the improved cost-efficiency, coverage, accuracy, comparability and timeliness of dissemination of the census results.
Programme performance in 2025
Member States adopt enhanced global statistical standards

20.51	A harmonized approach to statistical production helps to ensure comparability, efficiency and quality across national statistical systems. The subprogramme developed the Generic Statistical Business Process Model, which provides a standard framework and harmonized terminology to help statistical organizations to modernize their statistical production processes, as well as to share methods and components. To address the evolving needs and users’ feedback, the subprogramme facilitated a consultative process under the auspices of the High-level Group for the Modernization of Official Statistics, to update the existing model, including by ensuring alignment with modernization priorities – for example, increasing the use of new data sources and modern technologies – and promoting the interregional engagement of key stakeholders. The subprogramme also supports the roll-out of the model by member States.

Result 3: enhanced availability and accessibility of climate change-related statistics – Proposed programme plan for 2027

20.57	As the impact of climate change on economies and societies evolves, policymakers require timely, reliable and comparable climate-related statistics to inform effective decision-making. The subprogramme has supported national statistical offices in the production of evidence for policymaking by developing methodological guidance, building capacities and facilitating the exchange of good practices. The report Data for Climate Action: How National Statistical Offices Can Contribute, published in 2024, was an examination of how official statistics could contribute to the reporting under the Paris Agreement by providing data for climate change mitigation, adaptation and just transition, informing the public and measuring climate-related costs, transfers and financial flows.
Lessons learned and planned change

20.58	The lesson for the subprogramme was that, while a wealth of relevant data were often already available, they were often scattered across statistical domains and not easily accessible or linked to climate change. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will promote dialogue and closer engagement between producers and users of such statistics to improve understanding of data gaps, needs and availability. It will also support member States by enabling the exchange of best practices and innovative solutions in the production, dissemination and utilization of climate change-related statistics, including establishing dedicated national climate change-related statistics platforms, aimed at improving the availability and accessibility of these data for informed decision-making and the general public.
20.59	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 20.VI).

Subprogramme 4 – Economic cooperation and integration
Objective
20.61	The objective, to which the subprogramme contributes, is to strengthen policies on innovation, competitiveness and public-private partnerships for sustainable development in the ECE region.
Strategy
20.62	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Support the development of normative tools that complement existing economic policy, such as good practices, policy recommendations, and guidelines and standards on innovation and competitiveness policies and on sustainable public-private partnerships and infrastructure finance;

(b)	Strengthen national capacities and institutions through demand-driven capacity-building activities and policy advisory services, for example, by undertaking analyses of innovation systems, policies and institutions, offering related capacity-building activities, creating regulatory frameworks and project pipelines, and supporting the use of a methodology to evaluate and score public-private partnerships and infrastructure projects in support of sustainable development;

(c)	Support policy dialogue and peer learning, including by convening workshops, seminars, networks and forums that will provide relevant stakeholders with opportunities to discuss cutting-edge themes related to innovation and competitiveness policies and to sustainable public-private partnerships and infrastructure finance;
(d)	Carry out analytical work on innovation and competitiveness policies and on the mobilization of financing for public-private partnerships and infrastructure in the region.
20.63	The above-mentioned work will help member States to make progress towards achieving Sustainable Development Goals 8, 9, 12 and 17 and is expected to result in:

(a)	Publicly available, freely accessible and continuously updated standards and normative instruments in the field of innovation, competitiveness, sustainable public-private partnerships and infrastructure finance;
(b)	More effective harnessing of the transformative power of innovation for competitiveness and for sustainable development in member States;
(c)	Increased mobilization of financing to bridge the infrastructure financing gap faced by member States.
Programme performance in 2025
ECE Transformative Innovation Charter adopted by member States

20.64	Innovation plays an important role in advancing implementation of the 2030 Agenda. In the context of rapid technological change and multifaceted, interlinked Sustainable Development Goal-related challenges, there is increasingly recognition of a more holistic and adaptive policy framework capable of steering innovation towards societal goals. The subprogramme, working through the ECE Transformative Innovation Network, engaged governments, experts and practitioners over several years in the analysis of innovation policy trends and good practices. That collaborative effort culminated in the development of the Transformative Innovation Charter. Since its adoption, the subprogramme has been working with member States to help to operationalize it and expand its use in national policymaking.

children.text: Result 3: public-private partnerships aligned with ECE standards and guidelines on public-private partnerships in support of the Sustainable Development Goals – Proposed programme plan for 2027

20.70	While private sector finance can help to scale up financing for sustainable and resilient infrastructure, there can be challenges in identifying and preparing bankable public-private partnerships and infrastructure projects that also contribute to achieving the Sustainable Development Goals. The subprogramme has been developing standards and guidelines on public-private partnerships, policy recommendations and best practices that enable member States to identify and prepare infrastructure projects aligned with the Goals and contribute to increased access and equity, environmental sustainability and resilience, economic effectiveness, replicability and stakeholder engagement in and ownership of the projects.
Lessons learned and planned change

20.71	The lesson for the subprogramme was that the identification and preparation phases of public-private partnership projects could benefit from the further integration of sustainability criteria, to ensure that projects are designed to advance long-term economic, social and environmental objectives, while being attractive to potential investors. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will enhance the normative toolbox through the inclusion of additional standards and guidelines and will identify and avail targeted policy recommendations and best practices, with the aim of enhancing member States’ ability to identify and develop public-private partnership projects that are sustainable and bankable, thereby facilitating the mobilization of private finance for infrastructure development.

Subprogramme 5 – Sustainable energy
Objective

20.74	The objective, to which the subprogramme contributes, is to ensure secure access to affordable, reliable, sustainable and modern energy for all and to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and the carbon footprint of the energy sector in the region.
Strategy
20.75	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Conduct analysis and develop guidance, normative instruments and tools that help member States to meet their commitments, including the 2030 Agenda and the Paris Agreement, working with them to build their capacities to identify barriers to resilient, low-emission and low-carbon energy systems, to assess options that facilitate energy for sustainable development, to reduce the environmental footprint of energy, to accelerate the deep transformation of the energy sector, to facilitate the sustainable management of resources and to devise just energy transition pathways;

(b)	Strengthen national capacities and institutions through demand-driven technical assistance, capacity-building and policy advisory services – working in partnership with other relevant ECE subprogrammes – on sustainable resource management through (i) the application of the United Nations Framework Classification for Resources and the United Nations Resource Management System, in particular for critical raw materials, (ii) the interplay of low-carbon technologies, (iii) the measurement of, reporting on and mitigation of methane emissions from coal mines and gas operations, (iv) the safe operation and closure of coal mines, (v) energy efficiency across buildings and industry, transport and other sectors, leveraging digital technologies and systems integration, (vi) and the building of resilient energy systems through just energy transition strategies that align environmental, social and economic goals within a democratic governance framework;

(c)	Promote and facilitate policy dialogue, training, peer learning, public-private collaboration and the access of member States to other international cooperation mechanisms – working in collaboration with other relevant regional commissions, in particular for subprogramme work recognized by the Economic and Social Council for its global relevance – with the aim of advancing policy implementation and the use of normative instruments on sustainable energy.
20.76	The above-mentioned work will help member States to make progress towards achieving Sustainable Development Goals 7, 9, 12 and 13 and is expected to result in:

(a)	Adoption and implementation of national policies and normative frameworks that integrate sustainable resource management, circular economy approaches and just energy transition approaches, promote low-emission and low-carbon technologies, sustainable infrastructure and more efficient and digitalized energy systems, and support the implementation of the 2030 Agenda and the Paris Agreement;
(b)	Application of instruments and tools to reduce the environmental footprint of the energy sector, the enhanced management of natural resources, and strengthened energy resilience and security;
(c)	Increased investment in the modernization of energy systems, supported by more efficient and digitalized networks.
Programme performance in 2025
Strengthened cross-border cooperation on energy connectivity

20.77	Integrated and interconnected energy systems can enhance reliability, reduce costs and enable the deployment of renewable energy solutions at larger scales. In Central Asia, where national energy profiles and sources are often complementary, expanding cross-border electricity trade has been beneficial for advancing the energy transition, promoting economic diversification and creating jobs. Between 2023 and 2025, the subprogramme convened a series of policy dialogues and technical capacity-building workshops for policymakers, transition system operators and strategic energy planners from more than 20 member States on cross-border infrastructure development and renewable energy integration. The dialogues facilitated the exchange of best practices, the alignment of approaches and the identification of synergies across existing initiatives. To date, eight member States (Georgia, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Republic of Moldova, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan, Ukraine and Uzbekistan) have completed in-depth analyses on cross-border cooperation on energy connectivity and infrastructure. Subsequently, three member States in Central Asia (Kazakhstan, Tajikistan and Uzbekistan) integrated cross-border cooperation measures into their national energy strategies, further strengthening the political commitment to expanding electricity trade across the region. Owing to the facilitated peer-to-peer engagements, the initiative has also gained traction beyond Central Asia, resulting in two member States (Georgia and Republic of Moldova) adopting new policies on cross-border energy connectivity.

Result 3: reducing greenhouse gas emissions in high-emission intensity industries – Proposed programme plan for 2027

20.83	Many energy- and associated greenhouse gas emissions-intensive industries may face barriers such as the lack of technology, infrastructure, regulations and investment required to reduce emissions. The subprogramme has supported member States in their efforts to transform industries in which emission reductions are most challenging.
Lessons learned and planned change

20.84	The lesson for the subprogramme was that a systems-oriented, holistic approach offered important advantages for addressing complex and interconnected policy challenges. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will further strengthen its role as a platform for integrated analysis and policy dialogue, including through the development of analytical models and tools that reflect holistic pathways for reducing greenhouse gas emissions. The subprogramme will also enhance cross-sectoral coordination and support member States in developing coherent policy frameworks that align industrial energy efficiency strategies with broader climate and sustainability objectives, while taking into account economic competitiveness and other economic implications.
20.85	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 20.XI).

Subprogramme 6 – Trade
Objective

20.87	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to strengthen trade facilitation and electronic business, regulatory cooperation and standardization policies, agricultural quality standards and trade-related economic cooperation for an increased impact on sustainable development in the ECE region and beyond.
Strategy
20.88	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Support the development of normative tools that complement existing international economic law and policy, such as good practices, policy recommendations, common regulatory arrangements, standards (including information exchange standards and commercial quality standards) and guidelines;

(b)	Strengthen national capacities and institutions through demand-driven technical assistance and policy advisory services pertaining to regulatory and procedural barriers to trade, trade facilitation and digital connectivity, and support the practical implementation of normative instruments;

(c)	Support international policy dialogue and peer learning to foster consensus to facilitate trade, including by advancing digital connectivity in trade corridors, enhancing sustainability, transparency and resilience and reducing waste along value chains in critical sectors, and mainstreaming a gender perspective into quality infrastructure;
(d)	Undertake analytical work and evidence-based studies on issues related to digital and sustainable trade in the region.

20.89	In addition, the subprogramme, in its role as the custodian of trade-related standards, in particular those pertaining to information exchange under the United Nations Centre for Trade Facilitation and Electronic Business, code lists of the United Nations Code for Trade and Transport Locations, and marketing and commercial quality control of agricultural produce (Working Party on Agricultural Quality Standards), will facilitate their continuous review and maintain their repositories and repositories of related guidelines.
20.90	The above-mentioned work will help member States to make progress towards achieving Sustainable Development Goals 2, 5, 8, 9, 12 and 17 and is expected to result in:
(a)	More sustainable, inclusive and resilient cross-border trade, based on simplified, transparent, digitalized and harmonized global standards and procedures;
(b)	Improved consumption and production patterns along value chains based on increased transparency and traceability, and a reduction in waste.
Programme performance in 2025
Adopted gender action plans promote inclusive quality infrastructure for trade

20.91	Gender equality in quality infrastructure helps to ensure that standards, technical regulations and conformity assessment procedures reflect the needs of all users and that all persons can participate equally in shaping trade and regulatory environments. However, implications related to gender equality can be overlooked in the design of standards and quality policies. To address this gap, the subprogramme provided targeted support to quality infrastructure institutions in developing gender action plans, which set measurable objectives and benchmarks on equal participation in technical committees, gender-responsive outputs and inclusive workplace culture. This process has supported the enhanced, systematic integration of considerations related to gender equality into quality infrastructure policies and practices, strengthened institutional accountability, and supported greater alignment with broader national equality commitments.
20.92	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 20.XII).
Figure 20.XII – Performance measure: number of national quality infrastructure agencies that adopted gender action plans (cumulative)
Planned results for 2027
Result 1: enhanced digitalization of trade – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

20.93	The subprogramme’s work contributed to member States achieving an average implementation rate of 61 per cent for cross-border paperless trade facilitation measures, which exceeded the planned target of 60 per cent, and an average implementation rate of 83 per cent for paperless trade facilitation measures, which did not meet the planned target of 86 per cent. The target of an average implementation rate of 86 per cent for paperless trade facilitation measures was not met owing to methodological adjustments introduced during the 2025 iteration of the United Nations Global Survey on Digital and Sustainable Trade Facilitation, completed by member States, which resulted in a downward revision of targets achieved in 2023 and 2024, thus lowering the baseline for 2025.
20.94	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 20.XIII).

Result 2: improved transparency and traceability of value chains in critical sectors – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027
20.95	The subprogramme’s work contributed to 890 industry actors submitting 120 pledges, which exceeded the planned target of 870 industry actors and met the planned target of pledges.
20.96	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 20.XIV).
Figure 20.XIV – Performance measure: number of industry actors that have submitted pledges to traceability and transparency of sustainable value chains (cumulative)
Note: Pledges refers to the number of pledges submitted and industry actors refers to organizations involved in the pledges. Some of the pledges are submitted by a consortium/group of partners.
Result 3: agricultural quality standards revised with the aim of reducing food loss and waste – Proposed programme plan for 2027

20.97	Agricultural quality standards can help producers, traders and retail chains, and authorities to facilitate trade, improve production, maintain existing and reach new markets and protect consumers. To date, the subprogramme has facilitated the adoption of more than 120 such standards and, in recent years, promoted intergovernmental dialogue on ways to reduce food loss and waste.
Lessons learned and planned change

20.98	The lesson for the subprogramme was that its interventions were more impactful when food loss and waste considerations were systematically embedded in the review and development of agricultural quality standards. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will support the normative and standard-setting efforts of the Working Party on Agricultural Quality Standards to ensure that sustainability-related issues, specifically measures for reducing food loss and waste, are duly considered in the process of revising the existing agricultural quality standards and developing new ones. As a complement, the subprogramme will develop practical guidance on food loss reduction in value chains.
20.99	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 20.XV).
Figure 20.XV – Performance measure: number of agricultural quality standards revised with the aim of reducing food loss and waste (cumulative)

Subprogramme 7 – Forests and the forest industry
Objective

20.101	This subprogramme contributes to the objective of strengthening the sustainable management of trees and forests and enhancing the contribution of forests and forest products to sustainable development, climate action and biodiversity in the region.
Strategy
20.102	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Facilitate regional and global policy dialogue and promote transboundary cooperation for sustainable forest management, forest landscape restoration, sustainable use of forest products and services, including links between sustainable food systems and forests, and urban and peri-urban forestry;

(b)	Collect, validate and disseminate data and information, in-depth analysis and policy recommendations related to sustainable forest management and the sustainable production, consumption and trade of wood products, including wood energy;
(c)	Facilitate the exchange of national and subnational experiences and best practices and monitor progress in achieving sustainable forest management, including trade-related matters;

(d)	Build national capacity by providing demand-driven technical assistance, specialized expertise and policy advice on forest and the forest sector, including on crisis response and solutions for more resilient economies.

20.103	The above-mentioned work will help member States to make progress towards achieving Sustainable Development Goals 7, 11–13 and 15 and the implementation of the United Nations Strategic Plan for Forests 2017-2030, and is expected to result in:
(a)	More sustainably managed resilient forest ecosystems, which deliver vital goods and services that benefit society and contribute to climate mitigation and adaptation;
(b)	Increased and more sustainable use of forest products by member States, contributing to the circular and regenerative bioeconomy and the sustainable use of natural resources;
(c)	The integration of forest resources and products into cross-sectoral planning for the increased resilience of rural and urban ecosystems and food systems.
Programme performance in 2025
Enhanced integration of sustainable urban forestry solutions into national planning

20.104	Urban forests help to cool cities and can improve physical and mental health, reduce floods, attract people to the outdoors and businesses, and create jobs, among other benefits. The subprogramme supported member States, through the Trees in Dry Cities Coalition, to advance efforts to integrate urban forests into national planning and policy through targeted plans and mainstreaming, with the aim of advancing systemic, multi-pronged action in this regard. Specifically, it led the consultative process, which resulted in member States agreeing to a voluntary action plan and the subsequent development of dedicated guidance on scaling urban forestry with national policy and using it as a delivery mechanism for the Rio Conventions, further contributing to member States’ efforts to enhance urban forest cover through multi-level approaches.

Result 3: foresight-based planning for a resilient and sustainable forest industry – Proposed programme plan for 2027

20.110	The forest industry is influenced by shifts in market demand, regulatory frameworks and national economic models. In this context, the subprogramme developed a standard framework to gather statistical data and analysis on forest products and wood energy markets. The framework enables an assessment of the impacts of policies and market factors on the production of, trade in and consumption of forest products in the region.
Lessons learned and planned change

20.111	The lesson for the subprogramme was that its support to member States could be further strengthened by anticipatory and harmonized, evidence-based analysis. This allows for an assessment and the showcase of the role of the forest sector in national strategies and plans, including those pertaining to trade in response to market and regulatory changes, which have seen increases in pace and scale. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will support member States in applying the information made available through the standard framework for foresight analysis of the forest industry, which it will continue to maintain and enhance, as means of availing critical evidence for resilient forest sector management and anticipatory national development planning, taking into account evolving environmental and socioeconomic conditions.

Subprogramme 8 – Housing, land management and population
Objective

20.114	The objective, to which the subprogramme contributes, is to strengthen member State-owned and city-level programmes and policies promoting adequate, affordable, energy-efficient and healthy housing for all, smart sustainable cities, sustainable urban development and land management, and to advance evidence-based population and social cohesion policies.
Strategy
20.115	The subprogramme comprises two components: (a) housing and land management; and (b) population.
20.116	To contribute to the objective, the housing and land management component of the subprogramme will:

(a)	Assist national and local governments in defining, adopting and implementing sustainable urban, housing and land management policies and programmes through the development of guidance, policy recommendations and evidence and analysis on (i) affordable, accessible and energy-efficient housing for all, (ii) smart sustainable cities and digitalization in cities, (iii) innovative financial mechanisms for cities and the housing sector, (iv) sustainable land administration and management, including informal settlements, (v) the integration of climate change measures, including promotion of the climate neutrality of buildings, and (vi) urban resilience;

(b)	Promote the engagement of local and regional governments in the localization of the Sustainable Development Goals and the implementation of climate and other relevant global and regional commitments by facilitating exchanges of good practice, cooperation and dialogue among mayors, member States and relevant international organizations through the United Nations Forum of Mayors; and by supporting local authorities in the coordinated preparation of voluntary local reviews and in the application of commonly agreed indicator and monitoring frameworks;

(c)	Strengthen national and local capacities through (i) demand-driven technical assistance and capacity-building, (ii) peer learning and the exchange of best practices, (iii) advisory services and (iv) multi-stakeholder partnerships, to design and implement urban resilience plans and sustainable urban infrastructure projects, supporting also persons in vulnerable situations.
20.117	To contribute to the objective, the population component of the subprogramme will:

(a)	Collect, analyse and share data and policy information on ageing, gender equality and intergenerational relations to raise awareness on ageing, support evidence-based policymaking and monitor and appraise progress made in the implementation of the Madrid International Plan of Action on Ageing and its regional implementation strategy;

(b)	Support the development of national capacities to address policy concerns pertaining to individual and population ageing, including through technical advice and the sharing of good practices and lessons learned on advancing the mainstreaming of ageing across policy fields and levels of government;
(c)	Support regional cooperation and policy dialogue on ageing by providing an intergovernmental and multi-stakeholder platform for experience exchange, advocacy and peer learning.
20.118	The above-mentioned work will help member States to make progress on achieving Sustainable Development Goals 1,3, 5, 7–9, 10–12, 16 and 17 and is expected to result in:

(a)	National and local sustainable urban development and housing policies and programmes aimed at improving the affordability, adequacy and accessibility of housing, energy efficiency and the climate neutrality of buildings, and urban resilience;
(b)	Strengthened multilevel governance and the engagement of local and regional governments in policymaking;
(c)	Development and implementation by land authorities of sustainable national land administration and management systems;
(d)	Strengthened systems for the collection and application of quantitative and qualitative evidence on population and social cohesion;
(e)	Age-sensitive and inclusive policies that mainstream ageing across policy fields and levels of government.
Programme performance in 2025
Member States commit themselves to advancing housing affordability and sustainability

20.119	Rising rents and housing costs, combined with limited supply and speculative pressures, have affected households, especially of younger people and marginalized groups who, more often, continue to experience difficulties in gaining access to affordable and adequate housing. At the request of member States, the subprogramme coordinated a survey and, on its basis, prepared a report and a peer-to-peer exchange aimed at identifying solutions for improving housing affordability in the region. Subsequently, the subprogramme seized the momentum and facilitated ministerial-level discussions to strengthen commitments to prioritize equity and social inclusion; promote multilevel governance; expand affordable and sustainable housing through retrofitting and the construction of climate‑resilient infrastructure and efficient building technologies; and regenerate urban areas through redevelopment and adaptive reuse.

children.text: Result 3: anticipatory regional policy framework on ageing – Proposed programme plan for 2027

20.125	The Rome Ministerial Declaration on Ageing (2022) entrusted the Standing Working Group on Ageing with leading the process of updating the regional implementation strategy for the Madrid International Plan of Action on Ageing, with a focus on societal adaptation to individual and population ageing, and the demographic transition more broadly. The subprogramme has been working with national policymakers and experts to identify policy priorities that address emerging and future challenges for ageing societies.
Lessons learned and planned change

20.126	The lesson for the subprogramme was that the updated regional implementation strategy would benefit from a fuller reflection on emerging economic, social and digital transitions and guide anticipatory policy responses to population ageing. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will facilitate literature reviews, stakeholder engagement and intergovernmental consultations by the member State-led task force; collect and analyse relevant evidence, good practices and policy solutions; and advocate for the mainstreaming of ageing-related considerations into sectoral social and economic policies to help to ensure a comprehensive and cross-cutting perspective.
